rationale_for_baseline_tier: A sudden FAST-positive focal neurological deficit (right facial droop, right hemiparesis, aphasia) of abrupt onset ~45 minutes ago is a hyperacute stroke — an immediate, life-threatening, time-critical emergency. T5 (call 000, priority ambulance to a stroke-capable centre / stroke code) is the only appropriate tier: reperfusion therapy is strictly time-dependent and cannot wait for any lower-tier pathway.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden facial droop, one-sided arm/leg weakness and/or speech disturbance (any FAST-positive sign)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — thrombolysis is time-limited (~4.5 h) and thrombectomy (~24 h) from last-known-well; minutes matter
escalation_edges.rationale: FAST positivity is an acute stroke until proven otherwise and mandates immediate emergency transport; delay loses salvageable brain.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This looks like a stroke. Call 000 right now, say 'suspected stroke', and ask for a priority ambulance to a stroke hospital. Tell them he was last completely normal about 45 minutes ago.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Declining consciousness, choking, or noisy/difficult breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — recovery position on the non-paralysed (left) side, keep nil by mouth, stay on the line with 000
escalation_edges.rationale: A large infarct can compromise consciousness and the airway; positioning protects against aspiration until paramedics arrive.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes drowsy or his breathing changes, gently roll him onto his non-weak (left) side, keep his mouth clear, give nothing to eat or drink, and tell 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Seizure or vomiting before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.rationale: Protect the airway and prevent aspiration; both indicate a deteriorating major stroke needing the arriving paramedics urgently.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he shakes/convulses or vomits, keep him on his side, don't put anything in his mouth, and let 000 know immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any new or worsening symptom while awaiting the ambulance
escalation_edges.rationale: Continuous deterioration is expected in an evolving large stroke; keep emergency services updated so response is adjusted.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Stay with him, watch him closely, and call 000 back if anything changes at all.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Prescribed anticoagulant stopped about 3 months ago because of cost
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Flag affordability to the paramedics, stroke team and GP so secondary prevention is sustainable; do NOT restart the anticoagulant in this acute telehealth moment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The adult son is the only person present and the patient is non-verbal and hemiplegic
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the son clear, simple, step-by-step instructions: call 000, unlock the front door, keep the patient seated/supported and nil by mouth, watch his breathing, and call back on any change.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult with dense hemiplegia — high fall and aspiration risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not attempt to walk or move him unnecessarily; keep him supported and nil by mouth until paramedics arrive.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now for an emergency ambulance and say 'suspected stroke' — ask for a priority response to a stroke centre. Note the exact time he was last completely normal (about 45 minutes ago) and tell the paramedics. Give him nothing to eat or drink. Keep him resting comfortably and stay with him, and unlock the front door for the ambulance.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a time-critical emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Record the last-known-well time, the atrial fibrillation history and cost-related anticoagulation cessation, and the deficits observed — hand these to the paramedics and stroke team; the GP should review secondary prevention and affordable anticoagulation after discharge.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a sudden FAST-positive deficit as non-urgent — labelling it Bell's palsy, being reassured by preserved comprehension, or advising a wait-and-see / next-day GP review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The reperfusion window closes, guaranteeing permanent, devastating disability or death from an otherwise treatable stroke.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising the family to self-transport to the emergency department by car instead of calling a 000 ambulance stroke code — delayed arrival, no pre-hospital stroke pathway activation, and unmanaged deterioration/airway risk en route.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Ischaemic versus haemorrhagic stroke, and the exact deficit, cannot be determined via chat.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as a time-critical stroke and escalate to 000 regardless of subtype, keep nil by mouth, and withhold all antithrombotics until hospital imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to gather more detail, or giving aspirin on the assumption that it is ischaemic.
